subject: Constitutional Law
lesson_title: Freedom of Assembly and Association (Art. 40.6.1°(ii) and (iii))

questions.question: Article 40.6.1°(ii) protects the right of citizens to assemble peaceably and without arms. What express limitation does the Constitution place on this right?

options.A: The right may be restricted only in times of declared national emergency under Art. 28.3.3°
options.B: Meetings may be prevented or controlled in the interest of public order and morality, and provision may be made by law to prevent or control meetings in the vicinity of the legislature
options.C: The right is restricted to trade union assemblies and political meetings registered with the State
options.D: The right may only be exercised in designated public spaces approved by the relevant local authority

questions.explanation: Article 40.6.1°(ii) contains two express constitutional limitations: (i) provision may be made by law for the prevention or control of meetings that are determined in accordance with law to be calculated to cause a breach of the peace or to be a danger or nuisance to the general public (public order and morality); and (ii) provision may be made by law for the prevention or control of meetings in the vicinity of either House of the Oireachtas. These are constitutional limitations, not merely statutory ones.

questions.question: In Dillon v Minister for Posts and Telegraphs (unreported, 1981, SC), the Supreme Court considered the right to peaceful assembly. The court held that:

options.A: The right to assemble is absolute and cannot be regulated by local authority by-laws
options.B: The right to peaceful assembly includes the right to use the public highway for the purposes of peaceful protest, subject to reasonable regulation
options.C: Peaceful assembly is only protected within licensed premises, not on public highways
options.D: The right to assemble requires prior registration with the Garda Síochána

questions.explanation: Irish courts have recognised that the right to peaceful assembly includes the right to use public spaces including streets and highways for the purpose of peaceful protest. The right is not absolute — it is subject to reasonable regulation (including conditions as to route, timing and manner) in the interests of public order and the rights of others. Prior notification is commonly required but prior authorisation (which could effectively create a prior restraint) would be more constitutionally problematic.

questions.question: Article 40.6.1°(iii) protects the right to form associations and unions. This constitutional guarantee protects:

options.A: Only trade unions and political parties registered with the State
options.B: The positive right to form and join associations, and the associated negative right not to be compelled to join an association against one's will
options.C: Associations formed for commercial profit only; charitable organisations are regulated solely by charity law
options.D: Only associations that have been granted legal personality under company law or the Charities Act

questions.explanation: Article 40.6.1°(iii) protects both the positive right to form and join associations (including trade unions) and the negative right not to be compelled to join. In Educational Co. of Ireland v Fitzpatrick (No.2) [1961] IR 323 and NUR v Sullivan [1947] IR 77, the courts confirmed the constitutional protection of freedom of association, including the right not to be forced into union membership against one's will (open shop vs closed shop).

questions.question: In NUR v Sullivan [1947] IR 77, the court considered whether legislation that compelled workers to join a particular trade union was consistent with Art. 40.6.1°(iii). What was the outcome?

options.A: The legislation was upheld as trade union membership is a necessary condition of industrial relations law
options.B: The legislation compelling workers to join a specific union was found to violate the constitutional right to freedom of association, which includes the right not to associate
options.C: The court held that trade union legislation is exclusively a matter for the Oireachtas and immune from constitutional challenge
options.D: The legislation was upheld under Art. 45 directive principles which encourage the formation of trade unions

questions.explanation: NUR v Sullivan [1947] IR 77 is a foundational case on the negative dimension of freedom of association. The Supreme Court held that the constitutional guarantee of freedom of association in Art. 40.6.1°(iii) includes freedom not to associate — a person cannot be compelled by law to join a particular organisation. Legislation requiring workers to join a specific trade union as a condition of employment was held constitutionally invalid as an infringement of this negative right.

questions.question: The Prohibition of Forcible Entry and Occupation Act 1971 criminalises the forcible entry of land or buildings. A group of protestors enters a corporation-owned building to protest housing policy, refusing to leave. The company seeks their prosecution. Does Art. 40.6.1°(ii) protect the protest?

options.A: Yes — all protests on any premises are protected by Art. 40.6.1°(ii)
options.B: No — the constitutional right to peaceful assembly does not extend to forcible occupation of private property; lawful restrictions on trespass are constitutionally permissible
options.C: Yes, but only if the protest concerns housing, which is a constitutional right under Art. 45
options.D: No — Art. 40.6.1°(ii) applies only to assembly on public land

questions.explanation: The constitutional right to peaceful assembly does not provide immunity from criminal trespass or forcible occupation of private property. The 1971 Act criminalises forcible entry and occupation, which represents a proportionate statutory limitation of the assembly right in the interest of protecting private property rights under Art. 43. The assembly right is not a licence to trespass — protesters enjoy the right to assemble on public land and to protest within the law, but cannot use Art. 40.6.1°(ii) as a shield for criminal occupation of private premises.

questions.question: Article 40.6.2° provides that laws regulating the manner in which the right of assembly and association is exercised shall contain no political, religious or class discrimination. What does this anti-discrimination clause require?

options.A: Assembly and association regulation must be approved by all political parties represented in the Oireachtas
options.B: Laws regulating how the rights of assembly and association are exercised must not discriminate between different groups based on their political, religious or class character — regulation must be facially neutral and applied equally
options.C: All political parties must register with the State as associations before they can exercise Art. 40.6.1°(ii) or (iii) rights
options.D: The prohibition on discrimination applies only to laws enacted after 1937

questions.explanation: Art. 40.6.2° requires that regulations of the exercise of assembly and association rights be content-neutral — they cannot discriminate between different groups on the basis of their political, religious or class character. A law that authorised Garda policing of trade union meetings but not employer association meetings, or that permitted religious assemblies but not secular protests, would violate this anti-discrimination requirement. The regulation must operate equally across all groups.

questions.question: The Electoral Act 1997 restricts the formation and funding of political parties and requires State registration. Is this consistent with Art. 40.6.1°(iii)?

options.A: No — requiring State registration of political parties violates freedom of association as it gives the State a veto over political organisation
options.B: Yes — registration and funding requirements are a proportionate regulation of the manner in which political parties exercise their association rights, pursuing legitimate aims (transparency, accountability), and do not prevent parties from being formed or operating
options.C: The Electoral Act 1997 is exclusively an EU law measure and is immune from Art. 40.6 challenge
options.D: Yes, but only because the Art. 45 directive principles require the State to support political organisations

questions.explanation: Registration and funding regulation of political parties is constitutionally permissible as a proportionate limitation on freedom of association under Art. 40.6.1°(iii). The aim — transparency in political funding, accountability of parties, ensuring parties are genuine — is constitutionally legitimate. The regulation does not prevent parties from forming or operating; it merely regulates certain aspects of their activity (particularly financial). Proportionate regulation of the exercise of the right does not amount to a denial of the right.

questions.question: In Meskell v CIÉ [1973] IR 121, the Supreme Court found that pressuring an employee to relinquish union membership as a condition of continued employment violated Art. 40.6.1°(iii). Which aspect of the constitutional guarantee was engaged?

options.A: The positive right to form new trade unions
options.B: The negative right not to be coerced into abandoning membership of a lawful association, which was unlawfully being interfered with by the private employer
options.C: The right to collective bargaining under Art. 45 directive principles
options.D: The constitutional immunity of trade unions from tortious interference

questions.explanation: Meskell v CIÉ [1973] IR 121 established both the constitutional tort principle and, on its facts, a violation of the Art. 40.6.1°(iii) freedom of association. The employer (CIÉ) pressured employees to sign away their existing trade union membership as a condition of retaining their jobs. This constituted coercion to abandon association — a violation of the negative dimension of freedom of association. The plaintiff received damages for this constitutional tort, illustrating the horizontal application of Art. 40.6.1°(iii).

questions.question: A Garda inspector, relying on the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 s.8, disperses a lawful protest because she believes a 'breach of the peace is reasonably anticipated'. The protestors argue this violated Art. 40.6.1°(ii). What is the correct constitutional analysis?

options.A: The inspector’s belief is conclusive; any Garda belief of anticipated breach of the peace justifies dispersal
options.B: The dispersal of a lawful peaceful assembly requires that the anticipated breach be genuine and imminent; a vague or speculative belief of future disorder is insufficient to justify the constitutional right’s curtailment
options.C: Art. 40.6.1°(ii) only protects assemblies that have prior Garda authorisation; unauthorised assemblies have no constitutional protection
options.D: The Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 is immune from constitutional challenge under Art. 28.3.3°

questions.explanation: The constitutional right to peaceful assembly requires that any dispersal or limitation must be proportionate and justified by a genuine, concrete and imminent threat to public order — not mere speculative or remote anticipation of disorder. A 'reasonably anticipated breach of the peace' under s.8 of the 1994 Act must be read consistently with constitutional requirements: the Garda’s belief must be genuinely held, reasonable on the evidence, and the disruption of the assembly must be the minimum necessary interference. Vague apprehension is insufficient.

questions.question: Does the constitutional protection for freedom of association under Art. 40.6.1°(iii) extend to the right of a trade union to take strike action?

options.A: Yes — the right to strike is explicitly guaranteed by Art. 40.6.1°(iii) as an aspect of freedom of association
options.B: No — the right to strike was held in Educational Co. of Ireland v Fitzpatrick (No.2) [1961] IR 323 not to be constitutionally guaranteed; the right derives from statutory immunity under the Industrial Relations Acts
options.C: Yes — but only for public sector workers; private sector workers’ right to strike is exclusively statutory
options.D: Yes — the Supreme Court in BATU v Murphy [2007] recognised the constitutional right to strike as a component of collective bargaining

questions.explanation: In Educational Co. of Ireland v Fitzpatrick (No.2) [1961] IR 323, the Supreme Court held that freedom of association under Art. 40.6.1°(iii) does not constitutionally guarantee the right to strike or to engage in industrial action. The right to strike in Irish law derives from statutory immunity granted by the Industrial Relations Acts (specifically the Trade Disputes Act 1906 and its successors), not from the Constitution. Union activity including strikes remains governed primarily by statute, not by constitutional guarantee.
